title: Wet-felted doorstop
subtitle: A solid merino ball, approximately 15 cm in diameter, filled with dried peas or rice for weight
excerpt: A solid wet-felted ball of merino wool, 15 cm in diameter, filled during felting with a small bag of dried peas or rice to give it enough weight to hold a door open. The ball is felted in two halves around the fill bag and joined before fulling.
sourceNotes: Wet-felted doorstops are a common beginner project in UK felting workshops, typically a rounded or ovoid form weighted with rice or peas. Method synthesised from standard wet-felting and resist-ball technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, used as the raw material for wet felting.
The final stage of wet felting where vigorous agitation compresses and shrinks the fibres into a dense, firm fabric.
A dry felting technique that uses barbed needles to mechanically tangle loose wool fibres into a firm felt without water.

content.text: A wet-felted doorstop is a solid
roving

ball built around a small bag of dried peas or rice, which gives it enough weight to hold a door open without sliding. The ball is formed in two stages: a loose pre-felt shell is made first, the weighted bag is inserted, and the piece is then fully
fulled
to a dense, firm finish. Merino at 19-21 micron gives the smoothest surface. Coarser breeds such as Corriedale felt faster and are equally suitable.

items.name: Merino roving, contrasting colour (optional, for surface stripes)
Dried peas or rice, in a small cotton bag

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the fill bag

Fill a small cotton bag with 250-300 g of dried peas or rice and tie the top securely. The bag should form a rough sphere or compact oval about 8-9 cm in diameter. If no cotton bag is available, wrap the fill in a double layer of cotton muslin and tie with string.
Step 2: Wrap the roving around the fill

Lay a length of roving on the table and place the fill bag at one end. Roll the fill bag in the roving, working in a spiral, covering the bag completely in at least three overlapping layers of roving. The roving should extend approximately 1 cm beyond the bag's surface in all directions. Wrap the form in horizontal layers and then vertical layers to cover any gaps, building up the roving to roughly 20 cm in diameter (the ball will shrink to approximately 15 cm when fully felted).
Step 3: Wet down the ball

Mix a few drops of olive-oil soap into warm water (around 45°C). Hold the roving-wrapped ball over a bowl and pour the soapy water over it slowly, turning the ball to saturate the roving evenly on all sides. Squeeze the ball gently with both hands to press out air and make contact between fibres. The ball will be heavy from the fill bag; support it on the felting mat while working.
Step 4: Begin felting

Roll the wet ball between both palms with light, even pressure. Do not squeeze too hard at first: the goal is to compress the roving evenly around the fill. After 5 minutes of rolling, the surface will begin to firm up slightly. Switch to rolling the ball on the felting mat with both palms, using more pressure. Continue for 10-12 minutes, working around the entire surface. Avoid pressing the same spot repeatedly, which creates flat patches.
Step 5: Full to density

Once the surface has formed a firm felt skin, increase pressure and roll more vigorously on the mat for a further 15 minutes. Re-wet with hot soapy water as the ball cools. Throw the ball onto the mat from a height of about 30 cm ten to fifteen times, rotating it between throws. The ball is
fulled
when it measures approximately 15 cm in diameter and feels dense and firm throughout. The fill bag should not be detectable as a separate object inside the ball.
Step 6: Rinse and dry

Rinse the doorstop in cool water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing. Place the ball on a dry towel and leave to dry at room temperature. The fill bag inside will also need to dry, which takes 24-48 hours in a warm room. Do not place on a radiator: felt can scorch. Turn the ball occasionally to ensure it dries evenly on all sides.

items.problem: The ball is not round, with one flat side
items.cause: Rolling was done predominantly in one orientation
items.fix: Ensure the ball is turned frequently during rolling, working in all directions across the surface. Roll from multiple angles: side to side, end to end, diagonally.

items.problem: The roving separates into patches during early rolling
items.cause: Pressure was too heavy before the fibres had begun to bond
items.fix: Begin with the lightest possible pressure and increase only once a surface skin is visible. If patches appear, press them back in with a wet finger and continue at reduced pressure.

items.problem: The finished doorstop feels soft and compressible
items.cause: Fulling was not completed; the felt has not reached full density
items.fix: Re-wet with hot soapy water and continue rolling and throwing for a further 15-20 minutes. Fully fulled merino should not compress more than 3-4 mm under firm hand pressure.

items.problem: The dried ball feels damp inside days after finishing
items.cause: The fill bag retained moisture and did not dry through
items.fix: Place the doorstop in a warm (not hot) airing cupboard for several days. A radiator shelf works well. The felt exterior prevents airflow to the fill; drying is always slower than for a hollow project.

content.text: Finishing and variations
Once dry, the surface can be decorated with
needle felting

: short lengths of contrasting roving are stabbed into the surface to form stripes, dots, or simple motifs. For a flat-bottomed doorstop, press the ball against the table firmly before it is fully dry to create a stable base. A simple loop of rope or a felt tab can be felted into the top during construction for carrying.
